identity.title: Frozen Cakes, Pies, and Other Pastries Manufacturing

lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market manufacturers of frozen bakery products other than bread, including frozen cakes, pies, doughnuts, pastries, desserts, and sweet yeast goods, supplying repeat external foodservice, distributor, retail, private-label, or contract-packing customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Frozen bread manufacturing, retail bakeries, fresh commercial bakeries, cookie and cracker manufacturing, snack bars, captive production without external customers, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat wholesale product supply priced per unit, case, shipment, negotiated program, or contract-manufacturing arrangement; revenue commonly flows through foodservice distributors, chains, institutions, supermarkets, and private-label customers.

a.bridge: BLS OEWS measures the broader four-digit NAICS 311800 industry rather than frozen cakes, pies, and pastry manufacturing alone. I treated forecasting, production planning, procurement, inventory and cold-chain exceptions, quality-document review, customer service, sales analysis, administration, and management as substantially AI-exposed; physical processing, decorating, packing, sanitation, refrigeration maintenance, warehousing, and transport as much less exposed; then judgmentally wage-weighted the groups. Frozen plants may be more automated and capital-intensive than the broader average, which would reduce not-yet-automated exposure, while cold-chain coordination may increase digital task exposure.
a.rationale: In broader bakeries, production occupations are 49.70% of employment and transportation and material moving are 13.23%. Management, sales, office support, business operations, planning, scheduling, and quality records offer the main AI opportunity, but much of product preparation, line operation, packing, sanitation, maintenance, and frozen logistics remains physical.
a.change_evidence: A 311813-specific occupation payroll file and plant task-time study split by automation level, measuring forecasting, scheduling, formulation, quality records, procurement, customer administration, cold-chain exception handling, and physical production tasks, would materially move the range.
caveats: OEWS combines all NAICS 311800 segments and excludes self-employed workers.
Published occupation counts do not reveal task time, current software use, or the wage share already automated.
The frozen subsector's lower provided compensation-to-receipts ratio suggests capital and materials matter more than labor, but that dataset input is not a task-exposure measure.

rho.rationale: Forecasting, scheduling, procurement, document review, and customer workflows are structured enough for meaningful deployment, but fragmented ERP and plant systems, cold-chain data, food-safety controls, customer specifications, and human verification slow implementation. AI assistance is more plausible than autonomous control of safety-critical production decisions.
rho.change_evidence: A 15-plant implementation cohort measuring sustained labor hours, forecast error, waste, service levels, cold-chain deviations, quality holds, and audit outcomes for twelve to twenty-four months would directly test realized implementation.
caveats: No 311813-specific AI implementation cohort was located.
Products vary between ready-to-eat and ready-to-bake formats, changing food-safety workflows and acceptable automation.

e.rationale: The industry manufactures packaged frozen products for downstream business customers, making repeat supply the normal model. Public manufacturer evidence documents foodservice, supermarket, distributor, private-label, and contract-packing channels. A minority may be captive, project-like seasonal co-packers, or too dependent on nonrecurring bids to qualify.
e.change_evidence: Customer-level invoice histories and contract reviews from a representative LMM 311813 sample, including affiliate sales, repeat purchase rates, customer tenure, seasonal programs, and co-packing terms, would directly measure eligibility.
caveats: The public-company evidence spans products outside 311813 and a much larger scale than the target firms.
The provided target-band firm count is margin-bridged and may be sensitive to this subsector's capital intensity and margins.

s5.rationale: Family and independent food manufacturers can sell for succession or strategic reasons, but frozen plants require meaningful equipment, food-safety systems, cold storage, and customer approvals, shrinking the buyer pool. Closures, asset sales, internal reorganizations, and family transfers without control change are excluded.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of PitchBook, CapIQ, GF Data, Axial, SBA 7(a), and specialist food-manufacturing adviser data tagged to 311813, screened for going-concern control transfers and matched to eligible firms, would materially refine the probability.
caveats: No source directly measures five-year qualifying control transfers for target-band 311813 firms.
Private transactions and plant asset sales are difficult to classify consistently.

q.rationale: Per-case pricing does not automatically pass through labor savings, but large foodservice and supermarket customers often buy without long-term commitments and choose on price, quality, demand, and service. Public manufacturer disclosures show contractual raw-material cost true-ups, rebates, competitive bidding, and volume loss, demonstrating mechanisms by which customers recapture productivity benefits. Differentiated branded products should retain more than private-label and contract-packed goods.
q.change_evidence: Five years of SKU/customer gross-margin bridges, contracts, rebates, bid histories, and price-volume data around labor-saving deployments, segmented by branded, private-label, contract-pack, distributor, and direct foodservice channels, would reveal realized retention.
caveats: J&J Snack Foods spans multiple snack and bakery categories and is larger than the target band.
Contractual ingredient-cost true-ups do not prove contractual pass-through of labor savings, so the value remains an estimate.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for broader NAICS 311800 from 62.1 billion chained 2017 dollars in 2024 to 74.4 billion in 2034, implying a five-year compound ratio of about 1.094. I center 311813 slightly lower at 1.07 because public manufacturer evidence showed pie volume declines and lost seasonal business, while retaining broad uncertainty because frozen convenience and foodservice demand can outperform fresh categories. The four-digit mix can bias the proxy in either direction.
d5.rationale: Broader bakery output is projected to grow 1.8% annually in real terms, supporting moderate demand growth. Frozen shelf-life, foodservice labor substitution, and centralized production support demand, but desserts are discretionary, customer programs can be seasonal, and public evidence shows that volume and bids can be lost even when nominal bakery sales rise through pricing.
d5.change_evidence: Six-digit real shipments and physical unit data for frozen cakes, pies, doughnuts, pastries, and foodservice bake-off products, segmented by retail, foodservice, private label, and contract packing, would materially narrow the range.
caveats: BLS output is four-digit and includes fresh bread, retail bakeries, cookies, crackers, and tortillas.
The J&J result is one company's nominal sales and volume commentary, not an industry quantity index.

o.rationale: Frozen pastries are physical foods requiring formulation, manufacturing, packaging, freezing, storage, safety controls, and distribution. Software can optimize or coordinate supply but cannot supply the edible product, and covered manufacturers retain accountable preventive-control and recall obligations. Demand may shift to customer-owned plants or alternative dessert formats, but that is limited software elimination.
o.change_evidence: Procurement data measuring shifts from outsourced frozen bakery suppliers to in-house production, local fresh preparation, imports, or non-bakery desserts, combined with facility automation and co-manufacturing trends, would test operator displacement.
caveats: Operator-required demand can move from independent target firms to integrated customers or larger manufacturers.
Ready-to-bake and ready-to-eat products have different handling and downstream labor requirements.

narrative.executive_view: Frozen pastry manufacturing has strong repeat outsourced-supply fit and durable physical-product demand, but its labor ratio is relatively low and factory work is dominated by production, packing, maintenance, warehousing, and cold-chain activity. AI's most credible contribution is in planning, quality documentation, procurement, customer coordination, and logistics rather than direct substitution for plant labor.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can improve demand and promotion forecasting, batch and labor scheduling, purchasing, formulation and specification search, quality-record review, inventory and cold-chain exception management, customer-service triage, bid preparation, and maintenance planning. Human accountability remains necessary for hazard controls, allergens, sanitation, line intervention, and product release.
narrative.value_capture: Per-case pricing permits initial retention, but private-label, contract-pack, distributor, and large chain customers can recover value through bids, rebates, pricing true-ups, renewal pressure, or volume shifts. Public evidence of contractual ingredient-cost true-ups and lost low-margin seasonal business supports a conservative retention range.
narrative.firm_availability: Most target firms should serve repeat external customers, though captive plants and seasonal one-off programs require screening. The plant, freezer, food-safety, customer-approval, and working-capital requirements may favor strategic buyers and make some distressed situations asset sales rather than transferable going concerns.
narrative.demand_durability: Frozen shelf life and centralized manufacturing support retail and foodservice convenience, while broader bakery output is projected to grow moderately. Risks include discretionary dessert demand, health preferences, private-label pressure, customer program turnover, alternative desserts, and share shifts to larger suppliers or in-house production.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key gaps are six-digit occupation and real-output data, target-firm transaction evidence, and observed AI savings and retention cohorts. Food safety, allergens, cold-chain failure, energy and ingredient costs, customer concentration, seasonal programs, recalls, capacity utilization, and capital spending can outweigh administrative savings. The provided compensation ratio mixes 2024 wages and 2022 receipts and is harmonized, while the firm count is margin-bridged rather than directly observed.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat foodservice, retail, and private-label supply combines long-lived physical demand with data-rich planning workflows.
narrative.principal_weakness: Low labor intensity and buyer power limit the economic importance and retained share of AI-enabled labor savings.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 311800
sources.supports: Reports broader-industry occupation employment and wages, including production at 49.70%, bakers at 21.90%, packaging-machine operators at 6.71%, transportation and material moving at 13.23%, and office support at 5.24%.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Bakers
sources.supports: Describes physical baking tasks and commercial production using high-volume machines and ovens that may be automated, alongside quality checks, cleaning, and equipment work.

sources.title: FSMA Final Rule for Preventive Controls for Human Food
sources.supports: Documents requirements for process, allergen, sanitation, monitoring, corrective-action, verification, supply-chain, and recall controls for covered food facilities, subject to exemptions.

sources.title: FDA Draft Guidance: Hazard Analysis and Risk-Based Preventive Controls for Human Food, Appendix 1
sources.supports: Identifies potential biological hazards for refrigerated or frozen ready-to-bake and fully baked bakery items, including distinctions involving fillings, frostings, and toppings.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 311813 Frozen Cakes, Pies, and Other Pastries Manufacturing
sources.supports: Defines the industry as manufacturing frozen bakery products other than bread, including cakes, pies, doughnuts, pastries, desserts, and sweet yeast goods, and identifies excluded adjacent industries.

sources.title: J&J Snack Foods 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Documents foodservice, supermarket, private-label and contract-packing channels; customer concentration and limited long-term contracting; competition on price, quality and service; contractual ingredient-cost pricing true-ups; bakery sales of $405.9 million; and pie volume and seasonal-bid losses.

sources.title: BLS Employment and Output by Industry, 2024-2034
sources.supports: Projects broader NAICS 311800 real output from 62.1 to 74.4 billion chained 2017 dollars between 2024 and 2034, a 1.8% compound annual rate.
